article.title: NEOPRISM-CRC: Nine Weeks of Presurgery Pembrolizumab Leaves All 32 Bowel Cancer Patients Relapse-Free at 33 Months
article.summary: A Phase II trial replacing post-operative chemotherapy with three cycles of pembrolizumab before surgery shows zero relapses at nearly three years in dMMR/MSI-high colorectal cancer.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A small but striking Phase II trial has produced one of the more compelling readouts in early-stage bowel cancer in recent years: every one of its 32 patients remains alive and free of disease 33 months after treatment, a result that challenges a treatment model that has been standard for decades. The NEOPRISM-CRC trial replaced the conventional sequence of surgery followed by several months of chemotherapy with three cycles of the immunotherapy drug pembrolizumab given before the operation — and, as of a follow-up analysis presented at the American Association for Cancer Research Annual Meeting 2026 in San Diego in April, not a single patient has relapsed, according to [ecancer's reporting on the AACR presentation](https://ecancer.org/en/news/28096-aacr-2026-groundbreaking-bowel-cancer-trial-follow-up-shows-zero-relapses).

## What We Know

The trial enrolled 32 patients with high-risk stage 2 or 3 mismatch-repair-deficient (dMMR)/microsatellite-instability-high (MSI-high) bowel cancer — a genetic subtype found in approximately 10 to 15 percent of stage 2 and 3 cases, representing an estimated 2,000 to 3,000 UK diagnoses annually, as reported by [ecancer](https://ecancer.org/en/news/28096-aacr-2026-groundbreaking-bowel-cancer-trial-follow-up-shows-zero-relapses). Patients in this group carry tumours with a defect in the molecular machinery that corrects DNA copying errors, making the cancer particularly susceptible to immune checkpoint blockade.

Instead of undergoing surgery first, followed by three to six months of post-operative chemotherapy, participants received three cycles of intravenous pembrolizumab at 200 mg before their planned resection, according to [Inside Precision Medicine](https://www.insideprecisionmedicine.com/topics/oncology/presurgery-pembrolizumab-may-be-the-future-for-some-operable-crcs/). After a mean follow-up of 33 months, none of the 32 patients has experienced a cancer recurrence, according to [Oncology News Today](https://oncologynewstoday.co.uk/groundbreaking-bowel-cancer-trial-follow-up-shows-zero-relapses/). That includes both patients who achieved a complete pathological response — meaning no cancer was detectable when the resected tumour was examined — and those who still had residual disease present at surgery.

Among patients who underwent surgical resection, 59 percent showed no signs of disease after treatment — a pathological complete response rate that would be considered exceptional in this population, [ScienceDaily reported](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260505234618.htm). Standard surgery combined with adjuvant chemotherapy carries an expected relapse rate of approximately 25 percent at three years, according to [Medical Xpress](https://medicalxpress.com/news/2026-04-bowel-cancer-immunotherapy-clinical-trial.html) — making the NEOPRISM-CRC results a marked departure from historical benchmarks.

The trial was conducted across four NHS sites: University College London Hospitals, University Hospital Southampton, St. James's University Hospital in Leeds, and The Christie NHS Foundation Trust in Manchester, as [Medical Xpress noted](https://medicalxpress.com/news/2026-04-bowel-cancer-immunotherapy-clinical-trial.html).

## Circulating Tumour DNA as a Response Predictor

A distinctive feature of the trial was its use of personalised blood tests that measure circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA) as a real-time proxy for how individual tumours were responding to treatment. Among the 25 patients with evaluable ctDNA data, 24 of 25 had detectable tumour DNA in the blood at baseline, according to [Inside Precision Medicine](https://www.insideprecisionmedicine.com/topics/oncology/presurgery-pembrolizumab-may-be-the-future-for-some-operable-crcs/). After the first pembrolizumab cycle, 24 percent of patients had undetectable ctDNA; by the second cycle, 43 percent; by the third, 58 percent. After surgery, ctDNA was undetectable in all 25 evaluable patients.

The research team used the pace of ctDNA clearance to define three molecularly distinct groups. "Super molecular responders" — six patients whose tumour DNA became undetectable after a single cycle — achieved a pathological complete response rate of 100 percent (6 of 6). "Dynamic molecular responders" — 11 patients who cleared ctDNA across the treatment course at varying rates — achieved an 82 percent pCR rate (9 of 11). "Poor molecular responders" — eight patients with persistently elevated ctDNA — had a pCR rate of 0 percent (0 of 8), as reported by [Inside Precision Medicine](https://www.insideprecisionmedicine.com/topics/oncology/presurgery-pembrolizumab-may-be-the-future-for-some-operable-crcs/).

Yanrong Jiang, a PhD student at the UCL Cancer Institute who is the first author on the study, described the blood-test results as closely tracking what pathologists later found in the surgical specimens: "When tumour DNA disappeared from the blood, patients were much more likely to have no cancer remaining, and this matched the long-term results we're now seeing. In addition, we also saw that immune profiling from tumour tissue, before patients start their first cycle of treatment, can help to predict response. We hope these tests may be used to guide treatment decisions in a more practical and timely way," according to [ecancer](https://ecancer.org/en/news/28096-aacr-2026-groundbreaking-bowel-cancer-trial-follow-up-shows-zero-relapses).

## What Researchers Said

Dr. Kai-Keen Shiu, the trial's Chief Investigator at the UCL Cancer Institute and a Consultant Medical Oncologist at UCLH, said the results build a case for rethinking how this patient group is treated. "Seeing that no patients have experienced a cancer recurrence after almost three years of follow-up is extremely encouraging and strengthens our confidence that pembrolizumab is a safe and highly effective treatment to improve outcomes in patients with high-risk bowel cancers," he said, as quoted by [ecancer](https://ecancer.org/en/news/28096-aacr-2026-groundbreaking-bowel-cancer-trial-follow-up-shows-zero-relapses). He added that the ability to stratify patients by ctDNA response could allow oncologists to adjust the intensity of treatment in both directions — reducing it for those doing well and intensifying it for those at higher risk.

For the super molecular responders, Dr. Shiu suggested the data may warrant exploring whether surgery itself could eventually be avoided in selected patients — an approach that has attracted growing interest in oncology for cancers where immunotherapy produces complete clinical responses, according to [Inside Precision Medicine](https://www.insideprecisionmedicine.com/topics/oncology/presurgery-pembrolizumab-may-be-the-future-for-some-operable-crcs/).

Professor Marnix Jansen, the translational research lead at the UCL Cancer Institute, said: "These results not only confirm the durability of responses we saw almost three years ago, but also provide crucial biological insights into why immunotherapy is so effective in this setting," as reported by [Medical Xpress](https://medicalxpress.com/news/2026-04-bowel-cancer-immunotherapy-clinical-trial.html).

## What We Don't Know

NEOPRISM-CRC enrolled 32 patients — a sample large enough to generate a compelling signal but too small to establish efficacy with the statistical power required for regulatory approval or to capture rare safety events. The trial has not yet been followed by a randomised Phase III study comparing the preoperative pembrolizumab approach against standard care head-to-head, and no timeline for such a study has been announced in material presented at AACR 2026.

The zero-relapse result, while striking, should be interpreted in the context of the trial population's characteristics. Patients with dMMR/MSI-high tumours are known to have a more favourable prognosis than microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer patients even with conventional treatment, and in the metastatic setting immunotherapy has already displaced chemotherapy as the standard of care for this subtype. Whether the presurgery approach offers advantages over simply treating with immunotherapy post-operatively remains an open question that a randomised trial would need to address.

The data cutoff for the follow-up analysis presented at AACR was March 31, 2026, meaning the zero-relapse result reflects outcomes as of that date; longer follow-up will be needed to determine whether the durability holds across a five- or ten-year horizon.

The tolerability data reported suggest the preoperative approach was generally manageable, though detailed adverse event rates from the primary publication have not been fully reproduced in publicly accessible reporting.

## Analysis

For a subtype that accounts for roughly one in eight to one in ten early-stage bowel cancer diagnoses, NEOPRISM-CRC delivers an unusually coherent early-efficacy picture. The zero-relapse readout at 33 months is particularly notable because it holds not just for the 59 percent of patients who achieved a pathological complete response — where durable remission would be expected — but also for patients with residual disease detected at surgery. If that pattern holds at longer follow-up in a larger trial, it would suggest the immunotherapy is producing systemic immune memory that continues to control microscopic disease even when bulk tumour elimination was incomplete.

The ctDNA stratification layer adds practical value beyond the efficacy signal itself. A molecular biomarker capable of identifying, within a single treatment cycle, which patients are likely to achieve complete pathological clearance would allow oncologists to personalise the number of presurgery cycles, potentially sparing poor molecular responders from a treatment that is unlikely to benefit them, and opening the door to watch-and-wait strategies in super responders. Whether blood-based response monitoring of this type translates reliably across broader, less selected patient populations is a question that Phase III design will need to address.
